questions.q: The modern trigonometric word 'sine' is historically descended from which Indian mathematical term?
questions.explanation: The Sanskrit term 'jya' (and 'ardha-jya', half-chord) is the direct ancestor of the modern 'sine'.

questions.q: In classical Indian mathematics, what does 'kojya' correspond to in modern trigonometry?
questions.explanation: 'Kojya' (the koti-jya) is the ancestor of the modern cosine.

questions.q: The Sanskrit term 'jya-ardha' or 'ardha-jya' literally refers to what geometric quantity?
questions.explanation: Indian astronomers worked with the half-chord (ardha-jya) of an arc, which is exactly the modern sine times the radius.

questions.q: In which language did Indian mathematicians originally write their trigonometric works?
questions.explanation: Classical Indian astronomical and mathematical texts were composed in Sanskrit.

questions.q: The Indian sine concept passed into world mathematics chiefly through translation into which language before reaching Latin Europe?
questions.explanation: Indian works were translated into Arabic, and from Arabic the sine function eventually reached medieval Europe.

questions.q: Indian astronomers primarily developed trigonometry as a tool to support which field of study?
questions.explanation: Indian trigonometry arose mainly to serve astronomical calculations of planetary and stellar positions.

questions.q: Which 5th-6th century Indian astronomer-mathematician is famous for tabulating sine (jya) values in his work?
questions.explanation: Aryabhata, in the Aryabhatiya (499 CE), gave one of the earliest known tables of sine differences.

questions.q: Aryabhata's famous astronomical and mathematical treatise is known by what title?
questions.explanation: The Aryabhatiya, composed around 499 CE, contains Aryabhata's sine table and other results.

questions.q: The Latin word 'sinus', source of English 'sine', originally means which of the following?
questions.explanation: Latin 'sinus' means a fold or bay, chosen by translators to render the Arabic word derived from the Indian 'jya'.

questions.q: In modern terms, the Indian 'jya' of an arc equals the radius multiplied by which function of the corresponding angle?
questions.explanation: Jya was the half-chord, equal to R times the sine of the half-arc's angle.

questions.q: The Greeks generally used which trigonometric quantity, in contrast to the Indian preference for the half-chord (sine)?
questions.explanation: Greek astronomers like Ptolemy worked with the chord of the full arc, whereas Indians used the more convenient half-chord.

questions.q: The Arabic word that translators eventually rendered as Latin 'sinus' arose from a transliteration of 'jya' into the consonantal form 'jiba/jaib'. What did the Arabic reader take 'jaib' to mean?
questions.explanation: The unvoweled Arabic 'jb' was read as 'jaib' (pocket/bosom), which Latin translators rendered with 'sinus', also meaning a fold or bosom.

questions.q: Indian astronomers also used 'utkrama-jya' (or 'sara'). Which modern quantity does this correspond to?
options: The versine (1 minus cosine, times R)
questions.explanation: Utkrama-jya, the 'reversed sine' or sara, corresponds to the versed sine, R(1 - cos).

questions.q: Aryabhata's sine table was constructed for arcs measured in units where a quarter circle is divided into how many parts (giving 24 tabulated values)?
questions.explanation: Aryabhata tabulated 24 sine differences at intervals of 225 minutes of arc (3.75 degrees), covering the quarter circle.

questions.q: Which medieval Persian/Central Asian scholar wrote extensively on Indian sciences and helped transmit Indian trigonometry into the Islamic world around the 11th century?
questions.explanation: Al-Biruni (973-1048) studied and documented Indian astronomy and mathematics, aiding transmission of Indian methods.

questions.q: Which 7th-century Indian mathematician, author of the Brahmasphutasiddhanta, gave an interpolation formula useful for computing sine values between tabulated entries?
questions.explanation: Brahmagupta (598-668 CE) provided a second-order interpolation method for sine values in the Brahmasphutasiddhanta.

questions.q: The standard radius (R) used in Indian sine tables, such as Aryabhata's, was approximately what value (in minutes of arc), chosen so the full circle measured 21600 minutes?
questions.explanation: Indian astronomers used R = 3438 minutes, the radius whose circumference of 21600 minutes matches the 360-degree circle.

questions.q: Which Latin term for the cosine, still echoed in some older texts, reflects the idea of the 'sine of the complement', mirroring the Indian relationship between jya and kojya?
questions.explanation: Cosine derives from 'complementi sinus', the sine of the complementary angle, paralleling the Indian koti-jya.

questions.q: The 12th-century mathematician Bhaskara II, who also worked with sine functions and gave results approaching the derivative of sine, authored which celebrated mathematical text?
questions.explanation: Bhaskara II (1114-1185) wrote the Lilavati and the Siddhanta Shiromani, advancing work related to sines.

questions.q: Which European translator/astronomer of the 12th century is traditionally credited with the Latin coinage 'sinus' when translating Arabic astronomical works?
questions.explanation: Gerard of Cremona's 12th-century Latin translations are commonly credited with introducing 'sinus' for the Arabic 'jaib'.

questions.q: The Surya Siddhanta, an important early Indian astronomical text, is notable in the history of trigonometry for containing what?
options: A proof of the Pythagorean theorem
The first use of zero as a number
questions.explanation: The Surya Siddhanta presents sine tables and trigonometric rules central to early Indian astronomy.

questions.q: In Indian usage, the term 'koti' (in koti-jya for cosine) refers to which part of a right triangle relative to the arc?
questions.explanation: 'Koti' denotes the perpendicular/complementary leg, so koti-jya is the sine of the complementary arc, i.e. the cosine.

questions.q: The Kerala School mathematician Madhava of Sangamagrama (c. 14th-15th century) is credited with what advance directly involving the sine function?
options: Deriving the infinite (power) series for sine and cosine
questions.explanation: Madhava developed infinite series expansions for sine and cosine, anticipating later European Taylor series results.

questions.q: Aryabhata's sine table is often described as listing not the sines themselves but which related set of values?
options: The successive differences of the sines
questions.explanation: The Aryabhatiya famously encodes the table as successive sine differences, from which the sines are accumulated.

questions.q: The 'jaib' misreading that produced Latin 'sinus' is a classic example of what kind of transmission feature of Arabic script?
options: Lack of a symbol for zero
Omission of short-vowel marks leaving consonantal skeletons ambiguous
questions.explanation: Arabic often omits short vowels, so the consonants 'j-y-b' (from jya) could be read as 'jaib', meaning bosom/fold.

questions.q: Which Kerala School text, attributed to Jyeshtadeva (c. 1530), systematically presented the sine and cosine series and is sometimes called the first 'calculus textbook'?
questions.explanation: The Yuktibhasa by Jyeshtadeva gives reasoned derivations of the sine and cosine series of the Kerala School.

questions.q: The infinite series for sine known to the Kerala School corresponds in modern notation to which expansion?
questions.explanation: The Madhava sine series matches x - x^3/3! + x^5/5! - ..., the modern Maclaurin series for sine.

questions.q: Nilakantha Somayaji, a Kerala School astronomer who used and refined sine series, authored which major work around 1500 CE?
questions.explanation: Nilakantha Somayaji wrote the Tantrasangraha (c. 1500), which uses and extends the school's trigonometric series.

questions.q: Varahamihira's 6th-century work the Pancha-Siddhantika is significant for trigonometry because it records which fact?
options: Some of the earliest Indian sine values and the relation sin^2 + cos^2 = R^2-type identities
questions.explanation: Varahamihira (c. 505 CE) recorded early sine values and relations equivalent to the Pythagorean identity scaled by R.

questions.q: Why was the Indian half-chord (jya) a practical improvement over the Greek full chord for astronomical computation?
options: It directly related an arc to a right triangle, simplifying calculations
It avoided the use of fractions entirely
It eliminated the need for the radius
questions.explanation: Using the half-chord ties an angle directly to a right triangle, making computations more direct than with full chords.
